current_fact.fact_text: On 12 March 1989, a young software engineer at CERN named Tim Berners-Lee submitted a modest proposal for an "information management" system based on linked documents. That dry memo became the seed of the World Wide Web, the invisible architecture behind almost everything we now do online.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

current_fact.match_reason: Both the fact and the episode explore how digital networks shape our lives, from the birth of the Web to our tangled relationship with always-online devices.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 2011, a hydrogen explosion ripped through a reactor building at Japan’s Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant, one day after a massive earthquake and tsunami hit the coast. It became one of the worst nuclear accidents in history, reshaping global debates about energy, risk, and resilience.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: The reactor blast is a literal, catastrophic "electric shock"; the episode’s title and high-voltage guitar energy echo the raw power and danger of harnessing electricity at scale.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 2003, the World Health Organization issued its first global alert about a mysterious new illness: Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome (SARS). That warning marked one of the earliest modern attempts to coordinate a worldwide response to an emerging viral outbreak.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: A global health alert triggered a vast scientific learning curve; the episode leans into research, adaptation, and the emotional side of slowly figuring out new challenges.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 1933, in the depths of the Great Depression, Franklin D. Roosevelt addressed the U.S. public in the first of his famous “fireside chats.” Speaking calmly over radio, he used a new mass medium to rebuild trust in banks and government, one intimate broadcast at a time.

episode.title: Morning Drops #01 - Timeless Legacy
episode.description: Gaia guides us through timeless music to start the morning with the right foot! The first episode of the Morning Drops series — classic sounds that never fade.
songs: Roberta Flack - Killing me softly with his song

queue.match_reason: Roosevelt’s fireside chats are a “timeless legacy” of radio’s power to comfort; this warm, classic-sounding episode mirrors that intimate, hopeful voice in difficult times.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 1930, Mahatma Gandhi set off on the Salt March, a 200-mile walk to the sea to make salt in defiance of British colonial law. What began as a small act of picking up salt crystals became a global symbol of nonviolent resistance and economic self-reliance.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: The Salt March was civil disobedience turned into a movement; pairing it with an episode about Rage Against The Machine highlights music and marches as parallel forms of protest.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 1968, the island nation of Mauritius declared its independence from the United Kingdom. This small volcanic island in the Indian Ocean, long shaped by waves of migration and colonization, began carving out its own political and cultural path.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: An island redefining its place in the world fits with a travel-inspired "moving meditation" through global sounds and shifting identities.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 1992, Mauritius completed a second political transformation by becoming a republic while remaining in the Commonwealth. In less than three decades, it had shifted from colony to independent kingdom within the Commonwealth, to a fully republican system.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

queue.match_reason: A sun-drenched island formalizing its republic status pairs well with an "endless summer" episode—both evoke warm coasts, evolving identities, and slow, confident transitions.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 1913, the future capital of Australia was officially given its name: Canberra. The chosen location was a compromise between rivals Sydney and Melbourne, resulting in a planned city whose name likely comes from an Indigenous word meaning “meeting place.”

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: A new capital designed as a "meeting place" mirrors a boat that drifts between coastal cultures; both celebrate cities and seas shaped by many shores.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 1940, Finland signed the Moscow Peace Treaty with the Soviet Union, ending the brutal Winter War after just over three months. The Finns ceded large areas of Karelia and other territories, but their unexpectedly fierce resistance had already reshaped how military planners viewed small nations under pressure.

episode.title: Morning Drops #15 - Unfreezing
episode.description: A short, honest Morning Drop with no grand theme — just four tracks, a bit of voice, and the decision to not wait for the 'right' moment. Room to breathe today.
songs: Jane Birkin & Serge Gainsbourg - Je t’aime… moi non plus
Bernard Badie - In Love (DJ Quad's at Night Mix)

queue.match_reason: The end of the Winter War is a literal and emotional "unfreezing"—a thaw after bitter cold—echoed by an episode about loosening up, breathing, and moving forward.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 1942, the Battle of Java ended with the Allied ABDA Command surrendering to Imperial Japan in Bandung. It was a pivotal defeat in the Pacific War, marking the loss of the Dutch East Indies and its critical resources.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

queue.match_reason: A story of defeat in war contrasts with an episode about refusing to surrender space; the loud guitars and defiant women mirror the enduring spirit that outlasts military losses.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 1947, U.S. President Harry S. Truman announced what became known as the Truman Doctrine, pledging support to countries resisting communism. In a single speech, he helped redraw the world into Cold War spheres of influence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: While superpowers carved up influence through doctrines, Afrobeat emerged partly as a critique of those very power structures; this episode’s political grooves counterpoint the Cold War speech.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 1999, three former Warsaw Pact countries — Poland, Hungary, and the Czech Republic — officially joined NATO. It was a symbolic pivot from Soviet influence to Western alliances, closing one chapter of the Cold War map and opening another.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

queue.match_reason: Poland’s shift into NATO is a geopolitical journey; the episode literally hitchhikes through Poland’s landscapes and sounds, connecting policy maps to lived experience.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 1993, North Korea announced its intention to withdraw from the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons, refusing international inspections. It was an early sign of the nuclear standoff that would dominate global security debates for decades.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

queue.match_reason: Where nuclear brinkmanship is power without touch, Muay Thai and boxing are power made visible and disciplined; the episode’s focus on controlled force contrasts with secretive arsenals.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 1967, General Suharto was inaugurated as Acting President of Indonesia, effectively taking power from Sukarno. The handover crystallized a dramatic political shift following mass violence, ushering in decades of authoritarian “New Order” rule.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful

queue.match_reason: A turbulent transfer of power is a storm in a nation’s life; this reflective episode about transitions and better weather ahead provides a hopeful counterbalance.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 1920, the Kapp Putsch began as the right‑wing Marinebrigade Ehrhardt marched on Berlin, trying to overthrow Germany’s fledgling Weimar Republic. The coup ultimately failed, largely because workers declared a general strike and shut the city down.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

queue.match_reason: The Kapp Putsch was stopped by collective action in the streets; this episode celebrates another kind of mass gathering—dancefloors where people claim space and power together.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 1811, during the Peninsular War, Marshal Michel Ney’s forces fought the Battle of Redinha, staging a skillful rearguard action that twice slowed a superior Anglo‑Portuguese army. His maneuvers bought precious time for the main French force to retreat in better order.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: A rearguard action is a strategic beginning of an orderly retreat; Sedna’s very first explosive episode is the origin point for everything that follows—both are about how you set the tone at the edge of change.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 1689, the deposed James II of England landed at Kinsale in Ireland, igniting the Williamite War there. Ireland became the stage for a larger European power struggle, with local communities caught between rival kings and faiths.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Under Scottish Skies
episode.description: Gaia shares a personal moment of transition — a shift in seasons, in rhythm, and in life. From this space of quiet change comes a musical journey through Scotland: not the usual names, but voices that speak from the edges. A personal ritual and a small act of curiosity: an invitation to notice what grounds us when everything moves.
songs: Paolo Nutini - Pencil Full of Lead

queue.match_reason: The Williamite conflict spanned Britain and Ireland, entangled with Scottish and Irish identities; a Celtic-tinged Scottish journey resonates with those shared, contested histories of the islands.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 1622, Ignatius of Loyola and Francis Xavier, co‑founders of the Jesuits, were canonized by the Catholic Church. Their order would become one of the most influential networks of educators and missionaries on the planet, blending spirituality, scholarship, and global travel.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: The Jesuits’ Spanish roots and global cultural influence pair naturally with an episode on Flamenco’s evolution—both tell a story of Iberian ideas radiating outward and transforming.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 1088, Urban II was elected pope, a leader who would later call for the First Crusade. His decision set in motion centuries of religiously framed warfare and cultural contact between Europe and the Eastern Mediterranean.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: Urban II’s call for crusades sent Europeans toward Middle Eastern medinas; this episode’s journey from medina soundscapes to African rhythms reframes those crossings as cultural exchange instead of conquest.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 1579, the Siege of Maastricht began during the Eighty Years’ War, as Spanish forces surrounded a strategically vital Dutch city. The siege became infamous for its brutality, but it also marked a key episode in the long struggle that would eventually create a new Dutch republic.

episode.title: Morning Drops #17 - Steel City
episode.description: Gaia takes us on a short trip through Sheffield's sounds — from electronic textures to raw guitars and sharp storytelling. Four tracks, one city, different edges of the same story.
songs: The Human League - The Sound Of The Crowd

queue.match_reason: A city under siege and a city of steel both hold layered urban stories; this episode’s focus on one city’s many sonic edges echoes Maastricht’s role as a multi-faced crossroads in European history.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 2003, when SARS was first flagged globally, it also triggered unprecedented collaboration between labs, hospitals, and data networks across continents. For the first time, scientists could share genetic sequences and clinical findings online in near real time, reshaping how we respond to disease.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

queue.match_reason: A public-health emergency reminds us how fragile breath is; this airy, spacious dub episode leans into lungs, rhythm, and the simple act of breathing in a quiet forest.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 2011 in Japan, the Fukushima disaster also sparked one of the largest citizen radiation‑monitoring efforts in history. Ordinary people built DIY Geiger counters, mapped contamination, and shared readings online, turning fear into data and community science.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: Autotune is a tool that subtly alters invisible signals; citizen Geiger counters do something similar with radiation, making the unseen measurable and sparking debate about control and transparency.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 2011, the Tōhoku earthquake subtly sped up Earth’s rotation by a tiny fraction of a second and shifted its axis by several centimeters. Giant quakes don’t just reshape coastlines; they literally change our planet’s spin and the length of a day.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: A quake that ripples through oceans and even Earth’s rotation suits an episode about immersion in water and rhythm—both invite us to feel how subtle shifts can move an entire world.

queue.fact_text: On 12 March 2011, the same tsunami that hit Fukushima also sent free‑floating debris and entire docks drifting across the Pacific, some later washing up on North American shores. Along with human objects came living hitchhikers: invasive species that had survived an interoceanic journey on a storm‑tossed raft.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: The image of tsunami-borne rafts and marine life traversing oceans resonates with an underwater Arctic journey among whales—both are about how the sea quietly moves beings and stories across the planet.

published.fact_text: On 12 March 1989, a young software engineer at CERN named Tim Berners-Lee submitted a modest proposal for an "information management" system based on linked documents. That dry memo became the seed of the World Wide Web, the invisible architecture behind almost everything we now do online.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: Both the fact and the episode explore how digital networks shape our lives, from the birth of the Web to our tangled relationship with always-online devices.
